triage_context.environment: Unknown. Treat any externally-reachable entry point as untrusted; flag trust-boundary assumptions explicitly in rationale.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read in required_attributes: loop bounded by attrs_depr but indexes attrs_req
preconditions: htmlTagLookup(tag_name) returns a non-NULL htmlElemDesc with both attrs_req != NULL and NULL-terminated attrs_depr
attrs_depr array is longer than attrs_req (libxml2 static tables contain such entries like applet, basefont)
Caller reaches required_attributes() on ElementDescription[certain HTML4 element]
findings.rationale:
Code at ext/nokogiri/html4_element_description.c:27-30 confirms the scanner's claim: line 27 checks attrs_req for NULL, but loop at line 29 tests attrs_depr[i] for termination while dereferencing attrs_req[i] at line 30. This is a copy-paste defect - the identical pattern in deprecated_attributes (line 53-57) and optional_attributes (line 79-83) correctly iterate the same array they index. Sibling functions confirm this is a known class of bug in the file. The call chain is get_description(htmlTagLookup(tag_name)) -> required_attributes, reachable via Nokogiri::HTML4::ElementDescription[tag]. Required attributes are accessed in normal Ruby usage, and libxml2's static htmlElemDesc tables do contain elements (e.g. 'applet', 'basefont', 'isindex') where attrs_depr is longer than attrs_req. No protection exists. However, the impact is bounded to rodata pointer reads on adjacent struct fields in libxml2's statically-computed descriptor tables, making it an information-structural issue rather than arbitrary memory corruption.

Severity derived: 3 preconditions (requires specific libxml2 static table entries + library dependency + correct element lookup), authenticated access required -> MEDIUM. Alignment with claimed severity: 0 (MEDIUM is justified).
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/html4_element_description.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: CSS #id selector emits unescaped, escape-decoded id value into a single-quoted XPath literal
preconditions: Untrusted user input flows into doc.css() selector argument
CSS identifier contains escape-sequence \27 (literal apostrophe after unescape)
The CSS selector is a HASH (#id) selector, triggering visit_id at line 170-173
No app-side defense that escapes the CSS input before passing to Nokogiri::CSS.xpath_for
findings.rationale: Code at lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb:170-173 shows visit_id: line 171 regex-matches #id, line 172 interpolates Regexp.last_match(1) directly into a single-quoted XPath string with zero escaping. The CSS parser in lib/nokogiri/css/parser.rb decodes CSS escape sequences including \27 -> U+0027 (apostrophe). An input like #x\27 or \271\27 =\271 yields xpath //*[@id='x' or '1'='1']. The attribute-equality path (visit_attribute_condition, line 175-209) defends with a concat() rewrite when quotes are detected, but visit_id has no such protection. Verified: visit_id interpolates the raw decoded value into @id='...' without any quoting. Reachable via doc.css(user_input) -> Nokogiri::CSS.xpath_for -> Parser visits :ID node -> visit_id -> raw interpolation. A Ruby application using doc.css(\"##{params[:id]}\") for ACL/filtering is directly exploitable to bypass the CSS-escape mechanism which also defeats naive app-side quote filters.
findings.owner_hint: lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb; no CODEOWNERS entry for CSS module internals (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: CSS class selector ~= includes value interpolated unescaped into XPath string literal in css_class
findings.rationale: Code at lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb:138 shows that the :contains() function path produces 'contains(.,#{node.value[1]})'. The scanner correctly identified that this is a potential hardening concern but then incorrectly reported it as a genuine XPath injection vulnerability. Three independent verifiers found that: (1) the STRING path retains source quotes from the tokenizer, preventing quote breakout; (2) the IDENT and xpath_attribute paths flow through the tokenizer's nmchar character class which excludes parens, quotes, and injection metacharacters; (3) no concrete reachable breakout was demonstrated or constructible. The scanner itself stated 'No concrete reachable breakout was found' in its own confidence_reason, and the tokenizer character classes effectively block every interpolation path into contains(.,...). This is a defense-in-depth hardening suggestion, not a genuine XSS/XPath injection vulnerability. Dropped under precision policy: the majority of verifiers (2/3) ruled it FALSE_POSITIVE, so the tie goes to FALSE_POSITIVE under precision noise tolerance. The scanner's own confidence of 0.2 and its acknowledgment that 'IDENT/attr paths cannot carry injection metacharacters' supports the FP classification. The one TP vote acknowledged the hardening gap but noted it was theoretical. Verdict is FALSE_POSITIVE -- the finding is a valid hardening recommendation but not an exploitable XPath injection.
findings.owner_hint: lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb; no CODEOWNERS entry for CSS module internals (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference in push-parser free when context never initialized
findings.rationale: Code at ext/nokogiri/xml_sax_push_parser.c:6-15 shows: line 9 dereferences ctx->myDoc BEFORE the NULL check 'if (ctx)' at line 12. This is a real NULL-dereference pattern. However, all three verifiers agreed this is FALSE_POSITIVE due to extremely narrow reachability: (1) The allocate method (line 26-29) wraps the Ruby object with DATA_PTR = NULL; (2) Data is only set to a valid ctx by noko_xml_sax_push_parser__initialize_native (line 75-101) which calls xmlCreatePushParserCtxt on success; (3) Ruby's GC only calls dfree during sweep when the Ruby object was fully constructed and reachable, which requires the normal allocate+initialize flow; (4) rb_undef_alloc_func on the class prevents direct Ruby-level .allocate calls. The only path is if .initialize raises between allocate and DATA_PTR assignment AND the partially-allocated Ruby object survives to GC sweep - but _initialize_native returns self at line 101 (never returns Qnil), so an init failure would raise before DATA_PTR is set, and Ruby wouldn't GC an object whose init never completed. Exclusion rule 3 (intended design / constructor pattern) applies. This is unreachable in normal Ruby object lifecycle.
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/xml_sax_push_parser.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: Transform passes unchecked NULL result document to wrap, causing NULL-pointer dereference
findings.rationale: Code at ext/nokogiri/xslt_stylesheet.c:332-353: line 332 creates empty rb_error_str, lines 333-334 set up error hooks, line 336 calls xsltApplyStylesheet, lines 344-345 clear hooks. The scanner claims the error check at line 347 (checking rb_error_str.empty?) only catches errors routed through xsltSetGenericErrorFunc, but xsltApplyStylesheet can return NULL via OOM or other paths that don't populate the error string. The key question: can rb_error_str be empty AND c_result_document be NULL? Yes, this theoretically is possible if xsltApplyStylesheet fails without calling the error callback. However, three verifiers ruled this FALSE_POSITIVE for practical reachability: (1) xsltApplyStylesheet failures that return NULL almost always route through the generic error handler in practice; (2) the NULL-wrapping path at line 353 requires BOTH NULL result AND no error string simultaneously, an extremely narrow race that is unlikely under normal conditions; (3) even if reached, the crash would be a nil return rather than a security vulnerability in the traditional sense. The most-cited terminate="yes" case is likely caught by the hooked generic-error handler as the scanner itself noted. This is a defense-in-depth gap (valid hardening concern) but not actionable as a real vulnerability. Exclusion rule 13 (missing hardening with no concrete exploit path).
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/xslt_stylesheet.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: canonicalize leaks c_namespaces and output buffer when a namespace string contains an embedded NUL
findings.rationale: Code at ext/nokogiri/xml_document.c:647-661: line 648 xcallocs c_namespaces, line 651 calls StringValueCStr on each namespace entry (which raises on embedded NUL), line 661 frees c_namespaces, line 662 closes c_obuf. If StringValueCStr raises at line 651 due to an embedded NUL, the raise propagates before lines 661-662, leaking both allocations. The scanner is correct that a leak exists in this code path. However, 3/3 verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE because: (1) the precondition requires the caller to supply a namespace list with embedded NUL bytes - this is an extremely unusual and unlikely input; (2) the leak is per-call bounded (not amplifying); (3) the call to canonicalize with NUL-containing namespaces requires direct C-level or FFI-level interaction, not normal Ruby API usage; (4) even if reachable, a bounded per-call leak from abnormal input is not a meaningful security vulnerability. Exclusion rule 2 (test-only/dead code) and 13 (missing hardening with no concrete exploit path).
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/xml_document.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: Quadratic backtracking in JRuby meta-charset encoding-detection regex on full untrusted document
findings.rationale: Code at lib/nokogiri/html4/encoding_reader.rb:59-80 shows detect_encoding. Line 63 gates on Nokogiri.jruby? (JRuby only, not CRuby). Line 64 matches on chunk. The IO code path (read at line 93-118) reads ~1KB chunks which bounds the regex input. The memory path: Nokogiri.HTML4::Document.parse -> EncodingReader.detect_encoding(chunk) at line 102. For CRuby, the SAX push parser is used (lines 71-78) and the regex at 64 is never reached. For JRuby, the regex at line 64 does have quadratic potential if chunk is the full document. However, three verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE: (1) CRuby never reaches this regex - the SAX push parser handles encoding detection without regexes; (2) the JRuby path only triggers if Nokogiri.jruby? is true AND the full document is passed to detect_encoding; (3) even on JRuby, the regex requires the input to begin with '<meta ' and NOT contain 'charset=', and even then the quadratic backtracking is a DoS concern (exclusion rule 1, volumetric DoS) not an XSS/injection vulnerability; (4) the scanner's own confidence_reason acknowledged 'JRuby-only and quadratic (not exponential)' which further limits severity. The regex is legitimate but its vulnerability class and trigger conditions make it a false positive for security triage.
findings.owner_hint: lib/nokogiri/html4/encoding_reader.rb; no CODEOWNERS entry for Ruby library code (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: Unchecked xmlAllocOutputBuffer return dereferenced in canonicalize
findings.rationale: Code at ext/nokogiri/xml_document.c:633-637: c_obuf = xmlAllocOutputBuffer(NULL) without NULL check, then line 635-637 dereference c_obuf->writecallback etc. This is a genuine NULL-dereference if OOM. However, 3/3 verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE: (1) xmlAllocOutputBuffer returns NULL only under extreme OOM conditions; (2) there is NO attacker control over the trigger beyond inducing global memory exhaustion which is a volumetric DoS (exclusion rule 1); (3) the scanner's own confidence of 0.3 acknowledges this is 'low-severity, low-exploitability for a library trust boundary'; (4) this is a defense-in-depth robustness issue, not a concrete security vulnerability. Exclusion rule 1 (volumetric DoS/infrastructure-layer) and 13 (missing hardening with no concrete exploit path).
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/xml_document.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: size_t caret-diagnostic length truncated to int in rb_utf8_str_new
findings.rationale: Code at ext/nokogiri/gumbo.c:318-319: gumbo_caret_diagnostic_to_string returns size_t length, cast to (int) at line 319 for rb_utf8_str_new. The scanner correctly identified a signedness issue. However, 3/3 verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE: (1) the column value that drives the diagnostic length is bounded by a single HTML line; (2) to exceed INT_MAX requires the source line to exceed ~2 GiB, which is a volumetric precondition (exclusion rule 1: volumetric DoS/oversized input); (3) the truncated value feeds a copy of an already-allocated buffer (no realistic OOB beyond the volumetric trigger), as the scanner itself acknowledged; (4) even with the truncation, the practical impact is a Ruby string with possibly incorrect length, not an exploitable vulnerability. The scanner flagged a genuine C idiom concern but the exclusion rules explicitly cover volumetric-oversized inputs and the concrete exploit requires multi-GiB single lines.
findings.owner_hint: ext/nokogiri/gumbo.c; no CODEOWNERS entry for C extension sources (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: find_prev/next_newline rely on assert-only bounds for error_location within source buffer
findings.rationale: Code at gumbo-parser/src/error.c:387-401 and 406-418: find_prev_newline and find_next_newline scan from error_location with assert(error_location >= source_text) / assert(error_location <= source_end) guarding bounds (lines 393, 394, 412, 413). Under NDEBUG these compile out, and the while-loops could theoretically walk off the buffer. However, 3/3 verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE: (1) the same scanner reported this as 'defense-in-depth gap' with no concrete violating path - all tokenizer error sites set original_text.data from utf8iterator pointers into the same input; (2) the invariants currently hold and no violating path exists in the reviewed codebase (gumbo.c lines 304-338, where gumbo_caret_diagnostic_to_string is called with input_str/input_len from the original parsed document); (3) the scanner acknowledged 'no concrete reachable path' and 'no such path is presently reachable from the Ruby API'; (4) This is a pure defense-in-depth hardening gap (exclusion rule 13: missing hardening or best-practice gap with no concrete exploit path).
findings.owner_hint: gumbo-parser/src/error.c (vendored submodule); no CODEOWNERS entry for vendored dependencies (top committer pattern would need git history)

findings.title: :contains() argument passed through with limited but possible unsafe value sources
findings.rationale: Code at lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb:102-159 in visit_function: the :contains() path at lines 138-139 produces 'contains(.,#{node.value[1]})'. The scanner itself acknowledged: 'For a STRING argument the value retains its source quotes from the tokenizer and the string1/string2 patterns forbid an unescaped matching quote, so direct breakout is hard' and 'tokenizer nmchar character class excludes parens, quotes, and spaces, so no metacharacter reaches the interpolation.' All 3/3 verifiers agreed FALSE_POSITIVE: (1) the STRING path in the tokenizer retains source quotes preventing quote breakout; (2) IDENT and xpath_attribute paths flow through the tokenizer which restricts characters; (3) no concrete reachable breakout was constructed; (4) this is the scanner's own hardening suggestion masquerading as a finding. Exclusion rule 13 (missing hardening with no concrete exploit path).
findings.owner_hint: lib/nokogiri/css/xpath_visitor.rb; no CODEOWNERS entry for Ruby library code (top committer pattern would need git history)
